macro_segments.title: Currency Exchange and Debt Repayment: Validity of Obsolete Money

micro_segments.title: Dispute between Rav and Shmuel on repayment with changed currency
Rav Nachman's limitation: payment only if heading to that place
Rava's challenge via maaser sheni: cannot transfer sanctity to non-current money
Interpretation of bar Kochba and earlier kings' coins as examples
Distinction: current kings' currency similar to earlier kings if not in circulation
Resolution: מקפידות and non-מקפידות governments determine currency validity

macro_segments.title: Currency Exchange and Debt Repayment: Validity of Obsolete Money (continued)

micro_segments.title: Maimonides' inquiry on maaser sheni with Babylonian vs. Eretz Yisrael currency
Establishing money changers in Jerusalem for foreign currency acceptance
Description of Jerusalem, Abraham, and David-Solomon coin imagery

macro_segments.title: Maaser Sheni and Inaccessible Money: Special Requirements for Sanctified Funds

micro_segments.title: Babylonian and Eretz Yisrael currency exchange for maaser sheni
Tolerance for indirect transfer via animals when fruit too large

macro_segments.title: Flattening and Defacing Coins: Damage Without Physical Removal

micro_segments.title: Tosafot questions whether images on coins violate avoda zara laws

macro_segments.title: Coins and Their Value: Nominal versus Intrinsic Worth

micro_segments.title: Rava's question on coin enlargement: payment based on face value
Dispute on whether payment adjusts for buying power or precious metal content
Rav Ashi's resolution: distinction between טיבע (precious metal) and תרעא (market price)
Case of Rav Papa and Rav Huna: historical example of currency revaluation

macro_segments.title: Damage and Mazik: Throwing Money into the Sea and Indirect Loss

micro_segments.title: Rava: throwing someone's coin into the sea is exempt if retrievable
Condition: exemption only if water is clear and coin visible
Distinction: גזילה (theft) versus מזיק (damage) in retrieval scenarios
Tosafot: retrievability, not visibility, determines if destruction occurred

macro_segments.title: Maaser Sheni and Inaccessible Money: Special Requirements for Sanctified Funds (continued)

micro_segments.title: Contrapositive case: cannot transfer maaser sheni to inaccessible money
Rabba's distinction: maaser sheni requires צרות in hand, not merely existing

macro_segments.title: Flattening and Defacing Coins: Damage Without Physical Removal (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava: flattening a coin is exempt because nothing material was removed
Abstract principle: no damage without removal of material from whole
Limitation: filing (removing material) is חייב, hammering (rearranging) is פטור

macro_segments.title: Damage and Mazik: Animals, Slaves, and Lack of Visible Injury

micro_segments.title: Application to slaves: striking an ear causing deafness makes slave go free
Rava's position: deafness requires interior wound, hence חייב in damage
Rava: piercing an ear of a cow is exempt if market value unchanged
Question on parah adumah: doing work vs. piercing ear in context of sanctified animal

macro_segments.title: Burning Documents: Intrinsic Value and Indirect Loss

micro_segments.title: Rava: burning someone's loan documents is exempt; only paper destroyed
Rami bar Chama's challenge: if witnesses exist, document can be rewritten
Rava's response: even absent witnesses, document has no intrinsic value

macro_segments.title: Grama and Indirect Causation: The Gap-Filler Category for Exempt Cases

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi bar Chanina: Rava's cases reflect machloket Rabbi Shimon and Rabbis
Rabbi Shimon: דבר הגורם לממון כממון דמי (indirect loss counts as property)
Rabbis: דבר הגורם לממון לאו כממון דמי (indirect loss does not count)
Rav Huna's limitation: Rabbi Shimon only applies to items with original monetary value

macro_segments.title: Burning Documents: Intrinsic Value and Indirect Loss (continued)

micro_segments.title: Chametz stolen then burned: debate on דבר הגורם לממון (indirect loss value)

macro_segments.title: Grama and Indirect Causation: The Gap-Filler Category for Exempt Cases (continued)

micro_segments.title: Meimar: Rabbi Meir's category of גרמי to bridge the gap between פטור and חייב
Case of Rav Ashi: burning documents held to be גרמי, pay full document value
Rashi's question: why was Rav Ashi burning documents; possible minority liability
